data.title: Ayar Labs and Alchip Unveil Optical I/O Reference Design
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Custom ASIC design house Alchip and optical I/O company Ayar Labs have unveiled a reference design platform for AI ASICs with multiple optical die-die I/O engines based on TSMC’s COUPE technology.

A reference design platform is required since there are so many different components in each AI ASIC socket, potentially including interposer, HBM, 3D stacked compute and SRAM chiplets, passive components, organic substrate, and optical engines, Ayar Labs CTO Vladimir Stojanovic told EE Times.

“Someone needs to put all of this together,” Stojanovic said. “Partnering with both TSMC and Alchip allows us to place our optical engine right into this AI socket; this ecosystem partnership is extremely valuable, since it’s the only way we can build an AI socket that’s relevant for the high volume and scale that’s needed in these deployments.”

Typically, TSMC will fabricate chiplets and assemble them on the interposer, but then the chipmaker or ASIC service will have to design an organic substrate with the optical I/O engines and assemble the final design. A reference design allows the partners to “break ground” on how to fabricate this type of system and shows what can be achieved with optical engines, Stojanovic said.

The reference design platform shows two reticle-sized compute die with HBM and other chiplets, paired with eight optical I/O engines based on Ayar Labs’ TeraPHY chiplet. This design would allow 200-250 Tb/s bidirectional bandwidth from a single SiP, more than an order of magnitude better than current-gen large GPU packages, Stojanovic said. This will enable scale up, but also enables extended memory (since it’s close to HBM’s bandwidth) in a meaningful way, he added.

Optical engine

Ayar Labs’ optical engine is based on its TeraPHY PIC (photonic IC) chiplet, with two additional layers of chiplets assembled via TSMC COUPE technology. Two layers of electronic chiplet are on top, assembled via SoIC process which allows dense connections between die with minimal capacitance to preserve energy efficiency. This technology will scale to UCIe-A and beyond, Stojanovic said, for at least the next decade.

Connection out of the package is via a detachable connector.

“[The customer] needs to certify companies that can do the detachable connector, both manufacturing and assembly on top of the optical engine, but there are also a lot of components underneath,” Stojanovic said. “Once the wafer exits the fab, how do you prepare it, dice it, flip chip it into the package… it’s so important to be a part of that high-volume manufacturing ecosystem capability that is tailored around this form factor.”

While other form factors are possible, the path to manufacturability is a “long and winding road,” Stojanovic said.

“That’s why we focused on chiplets and fitting into the assembly and packaging roadmaps that our high-volume manufacturing partners have, while making sure our chiplets have the performance required to enable this breakthrough in AI scaling,” he said. “We see this bandwidth as something that will fulfill the needs of future AI systems.”

Ayar Labs produces known-good optical engines. The reference design platform includes test programs that enable testing of the assembly and control firmware for the module, facilitating its integration into the package.

“The customer needs support to be able to understand the reliability qualification and test processes, so we are very tightly partnered with Alchip to enable that whole package for the customer to access,” Stojanovic said.

Alchip will customize the reference design for its customers’ needs, along axes including the number of optical engines, different protocols, different ASIC designs and sizes, and more.

“We are excited about getting to this form factor, because there is no multi-chip package in the world that has this type of bandwidth potential, and radix, which is super important for AI applications,” he said. “Scale-up fabrics need massive connectivity from a single package to be able to flatten the network and achieve the latencies required for inference.”

Reference designs like this one could be the final puzzle piece that enables the optical I/O ecosystem required for broader adoption of the technology, Stojanovic said.

“Industry design cycles are a chicken-and-egg problem,” he said. “If I want to go to the next CMOS node, I ask my foundry for a list of IP blocks to include on my chip, then I go to my ASIC service and ask what varieties of chip they know how to integrate into packaging, and then I start architecting my system. So, unless they are already showing that they have these components proven to be integratable, it slows down the whole innovation cycle. We’re trying to unblock this innovation so architects see the value at the workload level.”

data.title: Materials Firms Eye India’s Semiconductor Packaging Opportunity
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Semiconductors dominated Indian headlines in early September, but the country’s ambitions rely on companies supplying the critical materials that hold these semiconductor chips together. Whether it’s wires, pastes, solders, or fluxes, global companies are eyeing the Indian market to provide these materials, which determine whether a chip can survive heat, conduct electricity reliably, and fit into advanced 2.5D/3D packaging.

While India has a strong base of local companies supplying soldering consumables for electronics assembly, the domestic production of chip-grade, ultra-fine, and high-purity materials required for advanced packaging remains limited. This leaves the industry reliant on imports and early-stage research and development (R&D) efforts.

“Bonding wires are needed in OSATs [outsourced assembly and test companies] to connect the integrated circuit (IC) with the substrate. They are the direct material inside the IC. Silver adhesive is used for die attachment between silicon chips and lead frames and provides high thermal conductivity and excellent electrical properties,” Agus and Fushimi, technical representatives at Tanaka Precious Metal Technologies Co., Ltd. explained. “It is also used in power semiconductors to mount silicon chips onto copper substrates directly. The adhesive paste is not pure metal; it contains resin, solvent, and additives for functionality. Sputtering targets are used in front-end processes to form thin films, including electrodes. These are made from precious metals such as gold, platinum, and palladium, and sometimes from other specialized alloy metals.”

The India Semiconductor Mission has attracted many global suppliers of advanced materials, who are now eyeing opportunities in outsourced assembly and testing.

Key players and strategies

Manufacturing in the country is still in its early stages. Companies such as Tanaka and Indium Corporation are stepping up their presence, betting that demand for bonding wires, solder pastes, and silver adhesives will surge sharply once semiconductor packaging and fabrication plants start production.

Indium Corporation, which already runs a factory in Chennai making high-end solder paste, also sees momentum building. “We are proud of our factory in Chennai, and we see the electronics industry, both printed circuit board assembly (PCBA) and semiconductor packaging, having a very bright future,” said Ross Berntson, CEO of Indium Corporation. “The immediate reason we needed a footprint here was logistics, since solder paste is perishable. The long-term reason is that we believe India will be a place of not only demand but also innovation.”

Tanaka, which claims to have more than 30% of the global bonding wire market share, says India is now firmly on its roadmap. “We see the opportunity to bring our complete ecosystem to India,” said Yutaka Ito, managing director of Tanaka’s Indian subsidiary. “Precious metals are highly functional materials but finite, so they must be recycled from e-waste and scraps into new materials. This closed-loop operation is the foundation of our business, and we believe India offers a huge business opportunity.”

The company established its Indian subsidiary, Tanaka Kikinzoku (India) Pvt. Ltd., in Mumbai in 2020, initially focusing on serving the automotive sector. It expects semiconductor packaging to become the next growth driver. Ito added, “Between 2026 and 2030, most OSATs will have functional plants and capacity will likely double.”

Indium focuses on alloys and fluxes developed with customers for specialized applications. “Our unique offering is not only our product but also the way we bring it to market,” Berntson explained. “Most of our products are developed in collaboration with a customer who has a real problem. Once that problem is solved, many others facing the same issue also adopt the solution.”

One such product is Indium’s Durafuse LT solder, initially created for low-temperature assembly. “It is now finding applications in AI packaging and telecom,” said Berntson. “We also see strong opportunities in e-mobility and thermal management.”

Challenges and opportunities

Both companies are positioning themselves for the move beyond PCBA. Tanaka supplies bonding wires in 29 types for different loop heights and strengths, including applications in silicon carbide (SiC) packaging. It also offers silver pastes with adhesion properties suited for copper lead frames. Indium, meanwhile, is preparing for demand in e-mobility and thermal management and is partnering with universities in India to build technical know-how.

Ito acknowledged that infrastructure challenges remain, particularly in setting up recycling-driven refineries, but expressed his optimism in dominating the Indian market by introducing its advanced materials through OSATs and fabs. “Stable electricity and pure water are essential to sustain refining capabilities,” he said.

Indium, on the other hand, is already looking to expand its India base beyond manufacturing into research and development. “In the future, I could see us having an R&D team here,” said Berntson. “One critical factor I already see is the willingness to collaborate. Customers here are ready to work through challenges and iterate with us.”

As India prepares for its large-scale semiconductor packaging facilities to start pilot operations, suppliers of bonding wires, solder pastes, and adhesives are laying down their stakes early. For companies like Tanaka and Indium, India is not just a sales market but a test ground for building future partnerships in materials technologies.

data.title: Wolfspeed’s Adam Barkley On Three Common SiC Misconceptions
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Wolfspeed recently announced the commercial launch of its 200 mm silicon carbide (SiC) material products (see story here), which it hopes will be a key development to help accelerate the industry’s transition from silicon to silicon carbide.

In the company’s announcement, it said the advancement reflects Wolfspeed’s commitment to pushing the boundaries of silicon carbide materials technology and delivers the materials foundation needed to ‘make the future of more efficient power conversion possible’.

In this video, Wolfspeed’s VP of power technology development, Adam Barkley, discusses three common misconceptions about silicon carbide technology, and he talks about Wolfspeed’s material and device production, plus he highlights the growing market applications for SiC.

This follows Wolfspeed CEO, Robert Feurle’s statement when the company announced its full fiscal year financial results for the year ending 29 June 2025, when he said, “Reflecting upon my first three months with Wolfspeed, I am more confident than ever in my decision to join the company and our opportunity to further strengthen our position in the industry. With our world-class greenfield and vertically integrated facility footprint, recent additions to the senior leadership team, and robust IP portfolio, Wolfspeed is well-positioned to be the global leader in silicon carbide technology.”

In a broader industry context, silicon carbide (SiC) is considered the dominant material in the wide bandgap (WBG) semiconductor market due to its superior thermal conductivity, high breakdown voltage, and efficiency in high-power applications. Devices based on SiC materials are widely adopted in power electronics, especially for industrial drives, renewable energy inverters, and electric vehicles. This is mainly due to their ability to operate at high temperatures and voltages, resulting in reduced energy losses and improved system reliability. This often makes them a preferred choice for energy and industrial systems that demand high efficiency and long-term performance.

For those not directly involved in WBG semiconductors, there are still some misconceptions about the technologies. In order to explore those misconceptions, EE Times recently chatted with Adam Barkley, VP of power technology development at Wolfspeed, to dispel three common misconceptions about silicon carbide technology, namely:

  1. That silicon carbide is an immature technology.
  2. That use cases are limited to automotive traction.
  3. That there’s no need for innovation in wideband gap packaging.

Video summary

The conversation begins with an overview of Wolfspeed’s background, noting its origins as a spin-off from North Carolina State University in 1987 and its vertically integrated business model, supplying everything from bare substrates to packaged discrete power products. Barkley confirms Wolfspeed’s position as the number one material supplier globally.

Barkley then explains the significance of the launch of Wolfspeed’s 200 mm SiC materials portfolio, in terms of achieving greater economies of scale and cost-effectiveness by printing more die on a larger wafer area, as well as enabling the use of more modern, highly automated fabrication tool sets.

He then highlights thekey points about SiC technology, primarily in the context of dispelling common misconceptions. On the first misconception, he notes the maturity and reliability of SiC, with commercial products released for over 20 years and a total development history of about 40 years. He states that reliability is proven, with Wolfspeed’s products having amassed over 15 trillion fielded device hours.

He then discusses SiC’s core technical advantages, and advantages over silicon for applications requiring a blocking voltage above 600 volts, mainly due to low conduction losses, fast switching and high thermal conductivity.

Then Barkley discusses applications beyond automotive. While automotive traction has seen recent hype and major sales, he said SiC’s adoption is widespread in industrial and energy, data centers, and high-demand e-mobility (such as electric aircrafts, VTOL air taxis, and delivery drones).

Finally, he talks about innovation in packaging in areas like thermal management, and new materials and techniques.

See also:

Wolfspeed Launches 200mm SiC Materials to Drive Next-Generation Power Devices

PCIM 2025: Wolfspeed’s New CEO on Innovation and Growth

Silicon Carbide Growth Pivots Around AI and Energy

Wolfspeed Gen 4 Silicon Carbide Technology: Advancements in High-Power Electronics

A*STAR IME Launches World-First 200mm SiC Open R&D Line

data.title: Tenstorrent Productizes RISC-V CPU And AI IP
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

AI chip startup Tenstorrent has begun productizing its RISC-V CPU and AI cores as licensable IP. Tenstorrent already has IP licensees, including LG and Hyundai, with most of the company’s bookings to date coming from IP deals, Aniket Saha, vice president of product strategy at Tenstorrent, told EE Times.

“We are the only company I know of that uses its own IP product in its own chips,” Saha said.

Contrary to the widely held industry belief that offering both silicon and its IP would create a conflict, this hasn’t been a problem for customers so far, in part because they are either customizing the IP or addressing different markets (specifically, automotive), Saha said.

“I’ve been pleasantly surprised that customers actually want the IP we are using in our own products because we are betting our company on the IP,” he said. “That has been an accelerant for the IP we are building.”

Tenstorrent will also license IP to companies planning to build silicon products that compete with its own workstation and data center hardware products, Saha confirmed. “Customers will often build things slightly differently,” he said. “They can build complementary products, even if they are in the same market.”

One emerging customer is planning to do just this – build something very similar to Tenstorrent hardware, but with different memory configurations and other design choices, Saha said.

“Even in that data center space where our main business is, you could build ancillary products and not compete with us because of different core counts or configurations, so we are not seeing that much conflict,” he said. “Even if there is some conflict, we’d get royalties in the back end – we’d rather win royalties in the back end than not win anything at all.”

Saha said he sees Arm as the primary competition for Tenstorrent’s IP products, largely because they are the incumbent, versus other RISC-V IP vendors like Andes. “We welcome more high-end CPU IP vendors, because that makes the RISC-V story stronger,” he said. “For RISC-V, and us, to succeed, we need credible high-end CPU IP companies.”

Competition also comes from ground-up in-house designs, Saha said, but mainly for mid-range cores or very specific implementations.

Differentiation for Tenstorrent IP products in general includes the company’s commitment to open-sourcing as much as possible and its willingness to let its cores be customized. IP companies don’t typically allow modifications beyond configuration options, because it makes the IP very difficult to support.

“We saw, starting with sovereign compute markets, a strong pull coming in for using the IP and being able to take full control,” Saha said. “If our customer wants to modify [the IP], we provide them the entire code base, the infrastructure that is required to modify, and build on top of that. We call it the innovation license, and this has quite a lot of company pickup right now.”

In general, however, customers will be steered away from customization, depending on the internal engineering resources available at any given time, Saha said.

“Because it’s early days, we’ve accepted some customers who wanted [us to make] modifications [to the IP],” Saha said. “But we will phase out doing the direct customization or heavy customizations. Whenever there is heavy customization, we are hoping the customer will pick it up.”

A lot of traction for IP products is coming from Asia, particularly China, with US interest “slow” so far, Saha said.

Ascalon CPU cores

The first generation of Tenstorrent RISC-V CPU, Ascalon, will have its IP productized in implementations between 10 and 20 SPECint2006/GHz. Released today are the largest and the third largest. The largest, Ascalon-X, will compete with Arm’s Neoverse V2 and V3 cores, Saha said. The third largest, Ascalon-S, competes roughly with Arm Cortex-A78.

Verified, validated subsystems with configurable clusters between 2 and 8 Ascalon cores will also be available. Ascalon interfaces are specified to be drop-in replacements for Arm (cores have both coherent and non-coherent interfaces).

Tenstorrent is moving to an 18-month cadence with generations of these cores, with the next generation, Babylon, set to improve performance, frequency, and PPA.

An automotive version of the CPU, Alexandria, adds a feature set for automotive functional safety. This also opens up markets like industrial and medical.

“Because [industrial and medical] volumes are typically low, Arm traditionally does not go after those sockets,” Saha said. “We see ways to aggregate some of that volume.” 

A development board, Atlantis, will feature an 8-core Ascalon-X CPU with a 50-W TDP. This development board will be used by OEMs, automotive tier 1s, and ISVs to begin porting software that requires high single-thread performance.

The Atlantis SoC may eventually be productized for the AI PC, but this is still under discussion, Saha said.

Tensix AI cores

The next generation of Tenstorrent’s Tensix AI cores, Tensix-Neo, will be released this quarter, Tanuj Arora, senior product manager for AI at Tenstorrent, told EE Times.

In contrast to the current generation Tensix-Blackhole core, Neo has adopted a cluster architecture that shares memory and NoC across four cores, rather than per core, to enhance area efficiency and utilization.

“This is the first time that we knew [in advance] we wanted to sell this as IP, so we built it in a more modular, flexible manner,” Arora said.

This flexibility extends to specifying L1 cache sizes and data type support, among other things.

Similar to the Ascalon CPU cores, customers will handle simpler configurations, while Tenstorrent will handle customizations beyond these parameters. Customers who want to do this in-house can take an innovation license.

“[For innovation license customers], we provide the RTL, the verification infrastructure, and test benches that we’ve built, and then it’s on them to do it,” Arora said. “But that’s a challenging job, so we’ll hand-pick those deals carefully.”

  • The article was edited on 9/26/25 as Tenstorrent asked us to remove commercially sensitive information disclosed in error.

data.title: Plugging the Electronics Skills Shortage the U.K. Way With UKESF
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

If there’s one thing we often hear when people in the industry talk about skills shortages, it’s that too few young people are pursuing careers in electronics. So why is that and what needs to change?

In order to discuss this, EE Times spoke to Stewart Edmondson, CEO of a charity called the U.K. Electronics Skills Foundation (UKESF), in which he talked about a new skills charter to recognize employers taking actions to enlisting the next generation of engineers, an award to recognize outstanding teachers in electronics education, and about the girls into electronics. He also talked about the UKESF strategy for 2025 to 2030 which aims to address growing the skills pipeline for the U.K. electronics sector.

In talking to Edmondson, it’s clear that’s there’s no single silver bullet to solving the challenge of growing the pipeline of the next generation of talent for the electronics industry. It’s multi-faceted and needs to tackle all stages of the education cycle – from engaging with school-age children, to those at college or university, and even helping skills development for young people already working in electronics.

Developing talent pipeline is important if the semiconductor industry is going to meet its target we keep hearing about: to become a trillion-dollar industry by 2030. Whether it’s in the United States or the United Kingdom or anywhere else, the initiatives needed are essentially going to be similar globally.

First there’s the issue of awareness of electronics and semiconductors. The general public might recognize Apple or Amazon but may never have heard of Arm or ASML. So the companies providing the foundational building blocks are largely unseen, so both parents and children may never even consider what opportunities there might be within these electronics companies.

A big reason for this, according to Edmondson, is that the semiconductor industry is not good at promoting itself and what it does. This is not a new sentiment, as others have also said this (see interview with Shari Liss, executive director of the SEMI Foundation, last year). In order to address this, outreach programs are likely to help considerably.

In fact, the UKESF recognizes this, and this week launched its ‘Skills Charter’, which recognizes employers for their commitment to supporting undergraduates to gain industry experience, to support employees in promoting electronics to young people through outreach and engagement activities, and commitment to making electronics a more inclusive profession. The skills charter has recognized 33 employers in the U.K. for addressing the three initiatives above. [A full list of those 33 companies is at the end of this article.]

Educating the educators

Then the next challenge is educating the educators. That’s where organizations like UKESF in the U.K., and SEMI in the U.S. have been looking at how to engage with them. The UKESF’s award for Inspirational Electronics Educators announced this week and sponsored by Synopsys aims to recognize educators and award them with a £500 personal award and £1,000 grant to further electronics education in their school.

Encouraging girls into electronics

The other big push by the UKESF is encouraging girls into the electronics industry. Last month, the charity shared its ‘Girls into Electronics 2025’ report, which outlined the results of its program aimed at inspiring teenage girls about the possibilities of studying and working in electronics. Now in its third year and supported by Apple, the 2025 program reached 506 girls aged 14-17 from 92 schools across the U.K, with one-day events across 15 host universities.

In the feedback from the program, it’s interesting to note that 56% of the girls who were not previously considering a career in electronics are now inspired to do so. At each of the events, the girls were given the opportunity to:

  • Hear a sample lecture from a senior female academic.
  • Hear experiences of students and engineers studying or working in electronics currently, including engineers from Apple.
  • Tour the university department’s labs and research facilities, and the cutting-edge work of the researchers and academics.
  • Receive a hands-on introduction to microcontrollers using the Grove Beginner Kit for Arduino, which they were able to take home with them.

Beyond this, UKESF is working with Apple on the Apple GiE (Girls into Electronics) Mentor Program, which has selected 43 UKESF Girls into Electronics participants for academic year 2025/2026. Each student has been allocated a mentor, a hardware technology engineer from the Apple team. The initial mentoring program will be for three months, starting this month, September 2025.

To watch the full interview with Stewart Edmondson (recorded at the inaugural Microelectronics UK exhibition and conference in London), see below.

EE Times’ Nitin Dahad (right) talks to Stewart Edmondson, CEO of the U.K. Electronics Skills Foundation (UKESF) about its initatives to plug the talent gap.

*Note: The full list of companies being recognized by UKESF in The UK Skills Charter include: Arm; AMD; Apple; Cadence Design Systems Ltd; Chess Dynamics; Codasip; Collins Aerospace; Cirrus Logic; CSA Catapult; Dukosi; EDA Solutions; Embecosm; Ensilica; IDEX Biometrics UK Ltd; Infineon Technologies UK; ITDev; Leonardo (Edinburgh); MAC Ltd; Nordson; Plextek; Qualcomm Technologies; Raspberry Pi; Renesas Electronics Europe; Renishaw; R-P-R; Sensata Technologies; Siemens EDA; STFC Microelectronics Centre; STMicroelectronics; Teledyne; Viper Innovations; Waters; ZF Automotive.

See also:

Does the Semiconductor Industry Have a Perception Problem?
The UK’s Role in Powering the Next Era of Microelectronics
Canada Needs a Multi-Faceted Strategy to Scale Chip TalentEducation Panel: How to Expand the Semiconductor Industry Talent Pool

data.title: UCIe 3.0 Adds DSP Support
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The ubiquity of chiplets and their diverse usages drove the formation of a consortium to develop and oversee standards, so it should come as no surprise that the latest update addresses a specific, emerging market segment.

In a briefing with EE Times, Debendra Das Sharma, UCIe Consortium chairman, stated that the updates in version 3.0 aim to address ecosystem demands and the requirements of the consortium’s 140 member companies, as well as the needs of high-performance computing (HPC) and artificial intelligence (AI) systems.

Two years ago, the consortium released UCIe 1.1 to address automotive compliance requirements, while 2.0 added support for 3D chiplets. Das Sharma said the release of 2.0 also addressed issues around testing and debugging, including concerns resulting from the reduction of bump pitches, as well as firmware upgrades. “That’s not an interconnect problem per se, but that’s a problem that needs to be solved,” he said.

UCIe 3.0 aims to double the bandwidth, Das Sharma said. “We basically double the data rate with the planar interconnect, and that’s because people can’t get enough bandwidth.” Getting more bandwidth is accomplished by shrinking bumps further. “If the bump is reduced, then my bandwidth actually quadruples,” he said. “But that requires more on the process side.”

As is often the case with semiconductor standards, UCIe 3.0 aims to achieve these bandwidth goals while maintaining a low power profile, which is a key concern for AI-intensive data centers. 

A specific segment addressed in this latest update aimed at DSP vendors to support new applications, such as connectivity between SoC and DSP chiplets. Das Sharma said there are major DSP vendors active within the UCIe Consortium that are interested in making better use of chiplets and need to address power conversion challenges for sensitive analog circuitry. “There was an overwhelming request for this kind of support.”

High-speed data transmission protocols between data converters can now be mapped to UCIe Raw mode of operation, which allows DSP vendors to use the UCIe standard without the need for separate PLLs, while avoiding the introduction of additional frequency noise in sensitive analog circuits. This is achieved by utilizing the existing raw mode with enhancements to the internal RDI/FDI interface and reusing the UCIe Retimer encodings to send periodic synchronization markers and parity.

By meeting the needs of DSP vendors, Das Sharma said UCIe addresses new market segments, including wireless infrastructure, software-defined radio, and radar systems.

He said it is expected that new segments will continue to become important for UCIe as chiplets are adopted in other applications, including laptops and handheld devices.

Otherwise, the update focuses on higher bandwidth density, with 48 GT/s for UCIe-S and 64 GT/s for UCIe-A, aiming to double the data rate to meet the continued demand for higher linear bandwidth density in SoCs used in HPC and AI applications with shoreline constraints.

Das Sharma said the increase in bandwidth density is coupled with efforts to lower power consumption. “We always find ways to reduce power with manageability.” Changes in UCIe 3.0 resulted in a linear increase in black and white density to 1.65x/2x for UCIe-S/UCIe-A, with similar power efficiency.

Power savings in UCIe 3.0 are achieved through runtime recalibration and L2 optimization, which enable power-efficient link tuning during operation by reusing initialization states and reducing idle power on the sideband.

Manageability infrastructure enhancements include early firmware download, sideband priority packets, extending sideband reach, an open-drain pin, and fast throttle/shutdown, ensuring that all participating chiplets throttle to the pre-negotiated level at a defined rate.

Das Sharma said that going forward, the UCIe Consortium is focused on establishing an open chiplet ecosystem and a ubiquitous interconnect at the package level to address a diversity of use cases across server AI, edge AI, networking, automotive, and consumer storage.

data.title: AI and Chiplets Prominent at TSMC OIP 2025
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The design tool offerings of the top three EDA houses showcased at TSMC’s annual U.S. event once more affirm the two defining trends in the current semiconductor landscape: artificial intelligence (AI) and chiplets. TSMC’s Open Innovation Platform (OIP) Ecosystem Forum brings together partners to demonstrate their design advancements of TSMC’s latest process nodes and packaging technologies.

The EDA trio’s announcements at the event encompass new AI flows and multi-die innovations relating to 3D IC, advanced packaging, and chiplets. The advancements in AI and chiplet realms are intertwined, paving the way for high-performance AI and HPC chips that serve compute-intensive workloads.

The EDA trio—Cadence Design Systems, Siemens EDA, and Synopsys—is working closely with TSMC to facilitate AI-driven circuit and system designs, as well as accelerate multi-die innovations, making chiplets a commercial reality beyond large semiconductor outfits like AMD, Intel, and Nvidia.

These collaborative pacts with the Taiwanese fab also reveal the 2026 product roadmaps for AI- and chiplets-related technology initiatives. Moreover, they provide a greater visibility on how TSMC’s technologies for multi-die stacking and advanced packaging are evolving in the age of AI and chiplets.

AI inside EDA flows

Cadence displayed its AI-driven design solutions for optimal power, performance, and area (PPA) on TSMC’s N2 process node. TSMC has validated these AI-driven features—such as automated design rule check (DRC) violation fixing assistance—to enable faster design closure and greater efficiency in the development of AI chips manufactured on its N2 process node.

Cadence also demonstrated the Innovus+ AI assistant on its digital flows to showcase the AI-driven implementation. Innovus+ utilizes AI tools to integrate RTL synthesis and implementation technologies into a cohesive platform, delivering robust PPA results while simplifying complex design workflows.

Then there is the collaboration between Synopsys and TSMS on silicon photonics. It aims to create an AI-optimized photonic IC flow for TSMC’s Compact Universal Photonic Engine (COUPE) technology, enhancing system performance and addressing multi-wavelength and thermal requirements in multi-die and AI designs.

Likewise, Siemens EDA is working closely with TSMC to successfully evaluate DRC productivity improvements using its Calibre Vision AI software. This AI-driven initiative analyzes and prioritizes DRC violations, thereby enhancing debugging efficiency.

The AI-optimized photonic flow for TSMC-COUPE enhances system design performance and addresses multi-wavelength and thermal requirements. (Source: Synopsys)

Chiplets progress report

The EDA toolchains for multi-die solutions enabling chiplets go hand in hand with TSMC’s 3D stacking and CoWoS packaging technologies. These EDA toolchains span from 3D IC technology for heterogeneous integration to silicon photonics for turbo-speed data transfer.

Cadence claims that its 3D-IC solutions support TSMC’s advanced packaging and die stacking configurations offered on the fab’s 3DFabric platform. 3DFabric offers 3D silicon stacking and advanced packaging technologies such as System on Integrated Chips (SoIC), Chip on Wafer on Substrate (CoWoS), and Integrated Fan-Out (InFO) to enable homogeneous and heterogeneous chip integration.

Cadence’s 3D IC tools are now available for TSMC’s N3, N2, and A16 process nodes. The other areas where Cadence is working closely with TSMC include bump connections, physical implementation and analysis for multiple chiplets, and smart alignment marker insertion.

Siemens EDA has also teamed up with TSMC on the 3D-IC front, enabling a design flow for the TSMC-COUPE technology. Next, its Calibre3D Thermal software is now certified for static thermal analysis of designs based on TSMC’s 3DFab technologies. Siemens EDA’s Calibre 3DSTACK Advanced software is also certified for targeted physical verification solutions.

Synopsys’ close collaboration with TSMC regarding multi-die solutions has also been a prominent highlight of the OIP event held in Santa Clara, California. For instance, its 3DIC Compiler was in the limelight for its ability to facilitate automated UCIe and HBM routing, TSV and bump planning, and multi-die signoff verification.

More specifically, 3DIC Compiler’s unified exploration-to-signoff platform supports TSMC’s SoIC-X technology. The support encompasses 3D stacked designs, silicon interposer, and bridge with CoWoS technologies.

data.title: Pasquale Pistorio: Architect of the Birth of STMicroelectronics
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Pasquale Pistorio, credited with the birth of the European semiconductor giant STMicroelectronics, has passed away at the age of 89 in Milan, Italy.

The legendary engineer-manager is primarily known for the successful merger of Italian semiconductor outfit SGS and the semiconductor division of France’s Thomson-CSF, and then for spending nearly two decades transforming this combined entity into a European semiconductor powerhouse.

Pistorio was also one of the first leaders in the semiconductor industry to advocate environmental causes such as ecologically sustainable manufacturing. He also took a special interest in fighting the “digital divide” and was a member of the United Nations Information and Communications Task Force dedicated to bridging the digital divide.

Pistorio began his career in an era known as the “Italian miracle,” although electronics were hardly a distinct subject at that time. After graduating in electrical engineering from the Polytechnic University of Turin in 1963, Sicily-born Pistorio aspired to work as an R&D engineer at Olivetti, a large company located 30 kilometers from Turin.

However, one of his acquaintances, Julio Colombo, Motorola’s representative in Turin, thought that this engineer, also an outgoing fellow, would excel in sales. So, he persuaded Pistorio with a job offer of 150,000 lire per month, about $100 today. Back then, Motorola offered discrete transistors, diode rectifiers, power transistors, and early integrated circuits (ICs).

A year later, Pistorio moved to Milan to join Motorola’s national representative. When Motorola opened its own office in Milan in 1966, he joined the American company as a transistor salesman the following year.

Over the years, Pistorio rose through the ranks and relocated to Phoenix, Arizona, when he was appointed as the Director of International Marketing in July 1977.

The following year, he was promoted to General Manager of Motorola’s International Semiconductor Division, where he was responsible for design, manufacturing, and marketing activities for all regions outside of the United States.

He was also the first non-American corporate vice president ever elected to the Motorola board. But then he began to feel homesick; moreover, his and his wife’s parents were getting old. So, when he was approached in 1980 for the CEO job at SGS Group, the only microelectronics company in Italy at the time, he decided it was time to go home.

SGS Microelettronica

His national pride also led him to take on the challenge of heading a struggling Italian company and reviving its sagging semiconductor business. Società Generale Semiconduttori (SGS) was bleeding money like many of its European counterparts. Pistorio pushed through several reforms; for instance, he convinced the workforce in manufacturing operations to move to a seven-day-per-week schedule.

By 1983, SGS Microelettronica had become the first European semiconductor firm to achieve profitability. At SGS, Pistorio began to focus on niche categories such as system-on-chips (SoCs) and electronically programmable read-only memory (EPROM). The latter led to a partnership with the semiconductor manufacturing division of France’s Thomson-CSF, known as Thomson Semiconducteurs.

While the two companies began working on a joint EPROM development, their respective government owners in Italy and France started to consider a merger between the two semiconductor companies. At that time, Thomson-CSF’s semiconductor operation had been losing money for years. Thomson-CSF had built its semiconductor operation by acquiring the U.S. chip outfit Mostek in 1985.

The 1987 merger between SGS and Thomson-CSF—initially facing skepticism and even ridicule—defined Pistorio’s legacy as a semiconductor industry star. He immediately began restructuring operations of the new company, initially named SGS Thomson. For instance, he shut down seven manufacturing facilities and started constructing a new fab in Grenoble, France.

Next, besides SoCs and EPROMs, the company made bold bets in areas like smart power and MPEG-2 technologies. In 1989, the company produced a new chip for Nokia that combined power supply and power management features into a single device. That enabled Nokia’s mobile handsets to achieve a standby battery life of over 60 hours.

Furthermore, the company made a risky bet on MPEG technology for decompressing digital video files for display on a television set. In 1993, it supplied MPEG decoder chips for set-top boxes of Hughes Network’s digital satellite television service. That made the company a market leader in set-top boxes and digital TV hardware.

Pistorio took the company public in 1994. In 1998, Thomson sold off its shares in SGS Thomson, and it was at this time that the company changed its name to STMicroelectronics.

In 2005, Pistorio stepped down as President and CEO; the company’s board honoured him with the title of Honorary Chairman for his contribution in laying the foundation of what eventually became STMicroelectronics.

But he never retired. That year, he also founded the Pistorio Foundation to support children in the most disadvantaged areas of the world, focusing on projects dedicated to education, nutrition, and healthcare. In 2007, he was named Chairman of the Board of Telecom Italia. Pistorio was also a member of the board of directors of carmaker Fiat.

However, his most notable passion, aside from STMicroelectronics, was environmental protection. He was drawn into the sustainable development cause after a debate with his then-student son about the role of enterprises in ecological reform. “Ecology is free,” he said.

However, Pistorio, being in the upper echelon of the semiconductor industry hall of fame, will be remembered as one of the most influential managers in European corporate history for architecting the merger that created STMicroelectronics.

In 1980, when Pistorio took charge of SGS, it had $100 million in annual sales. In 2005, when he left, STMicroelectronics was a $10 billion company with no debts. The numbers speak for themselves.

data.title: Cybersecurity Expectations Peak as GenAI Lands in the Trough
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The Gartner Hype Cycle for Supply Chain Strategy, 2025, reveals that while supply chain cybersecurity has soared to the Peak of Inflated Expectations, generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) has entered the Trough of Disillusionment, even as it simultaneously presents new threats to secure supply chains.

The report provides critical insights to help CSCOs make informed investments and strategic decisions by identifying emerging, hyped, and proven technologies.

Peak of cybersecurity hype

Supply chain cybersecurity, defined as an integrated and holistic approach to protecting operations from threats such as malware and ransomware, has seen its expectations surge as organizations increasingly recognize the necessity of safeguarding their complex supply networks. However, the ambitious promise of comprehensive protection faces significant barriers.

Adequate protection remains challenging due to several systemic obstacles. Organizations struggle with a fundamental lack of clarity regarding the budget and ownership required for identifying and managing cyber risks.

Furthermore, the sheer breadth of supply chain IT and cyber-physical systems requiring protection complicates implementation. Most critically, the large number of multitier partners makes visibility into and management of third-party cyber risk a daunting task.

Mark Atwood, Managing VP, Research, with the Gartner Supply Chain practice, emphasized the immense difficulty of oversight in this environment: “The large number of multitier partners in an organization’s supply chain has made managing third-party cyber risk a daunting task,”

He further noted that the growing use of GenAI among trading partners acts as an accelerant, increasing the risk of intellectual property leakage and data breaches. CSCOs should manage this third-party risk by first collaborating closely with their organization’s cybersecurity team to define security specifications, which must then be enforced through contractual requirements with high-value partners.

GenAI’s disillusionment

While GenAI—technologies capable of creating new content, strategies, and designs by learning from vast datasets—is being increasingly embedded into supply chain solutions, allowing organizations to experiment at lower cost and risk, the technology’s initial hype has deflated.

The report places GenAI firmly in the Trough of Disillusionment. This landing creates significant practical hurdles that organizations face when moving beyond pilot programs.

These obstacles include the complexity of integrating GenAI tools with existing legacy systems, profound concerns surrounding data security and the potential for intellectual property leakage, and a notable lack of clear governance frameworks needed to manage risks like “hallucinations” or ethical issues.

Many of these concerns directly feed into heightened supply chain cybersecurity risks. The disappointment suggests GenAI is not the immediate panacea many had hoped for.

“As more organizations grapple with the challenges of scaling GenAI pilots and integrating the technology into legacy systems, it will appear as less of a ‘silver bullet’ solution,” observed Noha Tohamy, distinguished VP analyst in Gartner’s Supply Chain practice.

Despite this setback for GenAI, the enthusiasm surrounding its potential, alongside the emergence of agentic AI, is positively impacting older technologies.

Machine learning (ML)-based AI is nearing the Slope of Enlightenment, rapidly moving past the pilot phase and being scaled across the enterprise to boost efficiency and resilience in areas spanning planning, sourcing, logistics, and manufacturing.

The reduced risk associated with adopting ML-based AI, thanks to the availability of established tech solutions and implementation frameworks, is paving the way for sustained, enterprise-wide transformation.

data.title: AI Drives PCIe Aspirations
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

As PCIe 8.0 aims to double the data rate yet again with its 2028 release, the PCI-SIG may also need to reconsider whether backwards compatibility as far back as 1.0 is feasible.

The aim for the next iteration of PCIe is to achieve a raw bit rate of 256.0 GT/s and up to 1 TB/s, delivered bi-directionally via an x16 configuration.

PCIe 7.0, released earlier this year, targets data-driven applications such as artificial intelligence (AI) /machine learning, 800G Ethernet, cloud computing, and quantum computing. Key features included a 128.0 GT/s raw bit rate and up to 512 GB/s bi-directionally via x16 configuration, utilizing Pulse Amplitude Modulation with four levels (PAM4) and Flit-Based encoding, as well as improved power efficiency.

Similarly, PCIe is also aiming to support those segments, including an effort to address automotive use cases, which has become a higher priority in recent years, as it aligns with the increasing adoption of Ethernet and Non-Volatile Memory Express (NVMe) within modern vehicles. 

Automotive remains an interesting, high-volume industry for PCIe adoption, Al Yanes, president of PCI-SIG, said in a briefing with EE Times. “It’s great that all these industry verticals can leverage PCIe, but at the same time, it doesn’t change fundamentally.”

Among the priorities for PCIe going forward are reviewing new connector technology, achieving low latency, and meeting Forward Error Correction (FEC) reliability targets. As always, there is a focus on developing protocol enhancements to improve bandwidth while continuing to emphasize techniques to reduce power consumption.

Yanes said requirements of AI platforms are shaping PCIe evolution to enable low-latency bandwidth for memory access as the primary connectivity between processing elements, such as CPUs, GPUs, or accelerators. CPU scale-up is especially latency-sensitive and needs high bandwidth, he said, and PCIe also needs to maintain its doubling cadence to support network scale-out. 

In the meantime, PCIe 6.0 compliance testing is on schedule, Yanes said. Typically, an integrator’s list is finalized three years after the full specification is release, while preliminary FYI testing takes place two years after revision 1.0. Even then, PCIe specifications are considered living documents and have multiple versions / Engineering Change Requests (ECNs), although there is one compliance program for each specification revision.

Beyond the specification itself, the PCI-SIG took a step in June 2025 to release an optical-aware retimer as part of an effort to standardize optical PCIe architecture in a manner that is easily transferable to existing PCIe 6.0 designs and devices, as reflected in the PCIe 7.0 specification. “We already are optical-friendly,” Yanes said, “but we want to take it to the next step.”

In May 2024, the PCI-SIG released the CopperLink Cable specifications to evolve well-established industry standard connectors, scaling for future PCIe technology generations, as well as meeting the needs of AI and machine learning, which require large packet transfers, Yanes said.

While some standards are upping their cadence to deliver updates more frequently – NVMe is now aiming to release updates once a year – PCIe is sticking to a three-year, evolutionary schedule, Yanes said. “It seems to work for the industry. It’s our sweet spot.”

The transition from NRZ to PAM4 in PCIe 6.0 was a “heavy lift,” but not typical, he added.

As PCIe continues to evolve and meet emerging application requirements, Yanes said it is getting harder to maintain backward compatibility with previous iterations. The question is whether the latest specification needs to be compatible with 1.0, or if it’s sufficient that 8.0 remains compatible with 6.0 and 7.0. “We struggle with it every time, and every time we manage to get through,” he said.

Version 0.3 of the PCIe 8.0 specification has received work group approval and is now available to PCI-SIG members. The SIG was established in 1992 and released its first specification in 2003, and now has 1,000 member companies.
